query: role of ultrasound in staging penile squamous cell carcinoma

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Staging -- Men with a clinically negative inguinal exam
retrieved_docs.content: The extent of staging is based on the risk of occult nodal metastases. Low-risk disease (PTis, Ta, or T1a, histologic grade 1): Patients should undergo surveillance rather than a pathological nodal assessment by DSNB or superficial inguinal lymph node dissection. Intermediate-risk disease (pT1a, histologic grade 2): Patients should undergo a namic sentinel node biopsy (DSNB) or superficial inguinal lymph node dissection. High-risk disease (>pT1b): Patients should undergo an inguinal lymph node dissection (superficial or modified) or a DSNB, depending on surgical expertise. CT, MRI, or inguinal ultrasound imaging may be useful in preoperative planning.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: This activity focuses primarily on squamous cell carcinoma, including its etiology, epidemiology, pathophysiology, histopathology, evaluation, differential diagnosis, and treatment. The importance of the primary tumor's pathologic stage and histologic features, as well as the presence of lymph node metastases, in determining prognosis and treatment planning for squamous cell carcinoma are discussed. Treatment options and appropriate follow-up are covered in depth.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: A medical oncology consultation is appropriate if the patient is a candidate for neoadjuvant chemotherapy. A radiation oncology consultation is appropriate for patients proceeding with radiation therapy to treat their primary tumor. Poor prognosis indicators include a higher primary tumor stage, perineural invasion, basaloid or sarcomatoid histology, distant metastatic disease, and more extensive lymph nodal involvement. Patients with such indicators should receive a palliative care consult.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Staging -- Anatomic Stage and Prognostic Groups
retrieved_docs.content: Stage 0: TiS or Ta, N0, M0 Stage I: T1a, N0, M0 Stage IIA: T1b, N0, M0; or T2, N0, M0 Stage IIB: T3, N0, M0 Stage IIIA: T1-3, N1, M0 Stage IIIB: T1-3, N2, M0 Stage IV: T4, any N, M0; or any T, N3, M0; or any T, Any N, M1

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: HPV status should also be determined. The potential role of various viral and immune biomarkers in diagnosing and treating penile cancer is still evolving. [62] Pathological staging is necessary for men with palpable lymphadenopathy and for those who are at high risk for metastases based on the pathological features of the primary tumor. The nodes can be evaluated by ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration, dynamic sentinel node biopsy, or an inguinal lymph node dissection (superficial or modified). [63] [64] [65] See StatPearls companion reference reviews, " Lymph Node Dissection " and " Fine Needle Aspiration ," for more information on these procedures. [63] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of the regional lymph nodes is required for accurate staging and prognosis. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Staging -- Men with a suspicious or positive inguinal exam
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with penile cancer presenting with bulky, fixed inguinal or pelvic lymph nodes have relatively low survival rates when treated with surgery alone and are candidates for a multimodal treatment approach or a clinical trial.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The presence and extent of regional inguinal lymph nodal metastases, as well as the tumor grade, have been identified as the most important prognostic indicators in determining long-term survival in men with invasive penile squamous cell carcinoma. [176] [177] This prognosis varies depending on the number of positive lymph nodes, the presence of unilateral or bilateral inguinal extension, and pelvic lymph node involvement. [176] [177] For this reason, evaluation of the groin and pelvis is essential in the metastatic workup of a patient with penile cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, the clinical assessment of inguinal lymph nodes is not entirely reliable. False-negative clinical evaluations of the inguinal region by palpation alone are common, reportedly between 9% and 60% of cases. False-positive assessments are also frequent. For this reason, imaging is often obtained in conjunction with a clinical exam. Computed tomography (CT) scan, MRI, or inguinal ultrasound can be used, as well as positron emission tomography (PET) scans. [61] However, even optimal imaging can miss micrometastatic spread to the lymph nodes, which occurs in about one-quarter of all cases. [49] This is why a histological examination of lymphatic tissue is needed and essential for proper staging in higher-risk individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Staging -- Men with a suspicious or positive inguinal exam
retrieved_docs.content: Obtaining an FNA is recommended for pathologic assessment. Low-risk disease (Tis, Ta, T1a): Patients with clinically suspicious adenopathy but a negative FNA should undergo an excisional biopsy for a definitive pathological evaluation. High-risk disease (>T1b): Patients with clinically suspicious adenopathy but a negative FNA should proceed with a superficial or modified inguinal lymph node dissection.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity for healthcare professionals reviews the spectrum of penile cancer, primarily focusing on squamous cell carcinoma, the most prevalent penile malignancy. The etiology, epidemiology, histopathology, diagnosis, staging, and follow-up protocols of penile cancer are also reviewed. The activity addresses various treatment modalities, emphasizing the importance of an effective interprofessional approach to managing this condition to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who underwent inguinal lymph node dissections and were staged at N2 or N3 should be seen every 3 months to 6 months for the first 2 years and yearly from years 3 to 5. They should have chest imaging (CT or chest x-ray) performed every 6 months for the first 2 years and a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis performed every 3 months for the first year and every 6 months for the second year.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The interprofessional team includes a primary care doctor or advanced care clinician, a urologist, a radiologist, a nuclear medicine physician, an oncologist, and specialty-trained nurses. The team may also include pediatricians in their discussions regarding the pros and cons of neonatal circumcisions. Nurses assist in patient and family education, monitor patients, and report issues to the healthcare team. They also arrange close follow-ups when needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Staging -- Distant Metastasis (M)
retrieved_docs.content: M0: No distant metastasis M1: Distant metastasis

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The TNM staging system developed by the American Joint Committee on Cancer is generally used for staging carcinoma of the penis. Currently, it is in its 8th edition and is used to define prognostic stage groups and to develop an appropriate treatment plan. [175]

retrieved_docs.title: Penile Cancer and Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Managing Lymph Nodes
retrieved_docs.content: Managing regional lymph nodes in penile cancer is quite important, as the presence and extent of regional inguinal lymph node metastases is the single most important prognostic indicator in determining the long-term survival of men with squamous cell carcinoma of the penis. Up to 25% of patients with nonpalpable lymph nodes harbor micrometastases. Several factors help predict the risk of microscopic inguinal lymph nodal metastases. Tis, Ta, and T1 tumors have a risk of metastases from 4% to 14%. T2 and greater tumors are associated with a significantly higher risk of metastases to the inguinal lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) is a key anatomical site within the male genital system and is the focus of specific diagnostic procedures that are applied when the prostate tissue has been partially excised. This organ serves as the anatomic location for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي), linking the disease directly to the prostate tissue. In addition, the prostate is identified as the location of a broader category of prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي), which functions as a parent classification for other male genital disorders. These other male genital disorders are themselves situated in the prostate and are closely associated with screening activities (التحصّي). Together, these relationships highlight the central role of the prostate in both disease manifestation and diagnostic evaluation, emphasizing the importance of targeted procedures and screening in the management of prostate‑related conditions.

This integrated framework organizes genitourinary cancers by organ, anatomical site, and malignant‑tumor grouping, and links them to diagnostic coding, surgical management, and screening strategies essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2531
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of other male genital organs is classified as a subcategory of penile cancer (سرطان قضيبي), indicating that malignancies affecting male genital structures other than the prostate are considered within the broader taxonomy of penile cancer. This hierarchical relationship clarifies the categorization of male genital tract cancers and can inform diagnostic, therapeutic, and epidemiological considerations in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2529
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures; male genital refer to examinations and interventions performed on male genital organs, and the prostate (prostata) is a partially excised anatomy that is commonly involved in such diagnostic procedures. The prostate (prostata) is also the anatomic site associated with metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Recognizing that the prostate can be partially removed for diagnostic evaluation and that it serves as the primary location for this malignant disease is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1835
retrieved_docs.content: Transrectal biopsy of prostate is classified as a diagnostic procedure within the male genital system, indicating its role in evaluating prostate pathology in men. This procedure is organized under the broader category of male genital diagnostic techniques and is noted to have subordinate or related child procedures that further refine its clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: the primary site of prostatic disorder and related male genital diseases. Common pathologies include benign prostatic hyperplasia (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد), prostatic enlargement (تضخم بروستاتي), and prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا, سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Therapeutic strategies comprise transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) for BPH and related enlargement, open prostatectomy (which may involve partial removal of adjacent structures such as the seminal vesicles), and transurethral prostate incision. Urinary tract procedures (عملية في السبيل البولي) frequently employ an indwelling catheter as a parent entity, and systematic screening (التحصّي) of male genital disorders emphasizes early detection and monitoring of prostate and related conditions, illustrating the integrated anatomy, pathology, and treatment framework in male genitourinary care.

retrieved_docs.content: Other male genital disorders are considered a subcategory of prostate disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) and are linked to the practice of screening (التحصّي). Recognizing that other male genital disorders fall under the broader category of prostate disorder and are associated with screening emphasizes the importance of appropriate diagnostic evaluation in the management of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 880
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical classification of neoplasms of the genitourinary system is organized to include a range of malignant tumors, among them primary malignant neoplasm of the areola of the female breast and cancers of other male genital organs. Within the category of cancers of other male genital organs, penile cancer (سرطان قضيبي) is identified as a specific subtype. This hierarchical structure clarifies how diverse neoplastic entities are grouped under broader classification schemes, facilitating accurate diagnosis, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic planning across both female breast and male genital tract malignancies.

retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Genitourinary System is organized as a subcategory within both cancer of other male genital organs and cancer of the testis, linking these specific malignancies under a common classification framework. Cancer of other male genital organs represents a broader group of malignancies affecting male genital structures, and within this group penile cancer (سرطان قضيبي) is recognized as a distinct entity. This hierarchical arrangement clarifies how individual genitourinary cancers relate to one another, supporting accurate diagnosis, coding, and coordinated patient management.

The prostate is the anatomic locus for prostate disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) and its subcategories, including BPH, enlargement, and cancer. Transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) is a surgical procedure performed on the prostate and is clinically associated with all of these conditions, serving both therapeutic (BPH, enlargement, cancer) and diagnostic roles. Additional male genital disorders are considered subcategories of prostate disorder, underscoring the prostate’s central role in male genitourinary health. Systematic screening (التحصّي) is linked to early detection of prostate‑related disorders. Urinary‑system interventions also encompass indwelling catheters, which are a type of urinary‑tract procedure (عملية في السبيل البولي) used to manage various urinary conditions. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1523
retrieved_docs.content: Operations by approach encompasses open prostatectomy, indicating that open prostatectomy is a specific surgical approach classified under Operations by approach. Both open prostatectomy and Transrectal biopsy of prostate are linked to عمليات البروستاتا (prostate operations), showing they belong to the broader category of prostate surgeries. Transrectal biopsy of prostate is also categorized as a diagnostic procedure for the male genital system, reflecting its role in evaluating prostate pathology. Clinically, عمليات البروستاتا are associated with خلل وظيفي في الانتصاب (erectile dysfunction), highlighting a potential adverse effect of prostate surgeries on sexual function. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical approaches, recognizing diagnostic options, and counseling patients about possible functional outcomes.
